main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Depth Pro: a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation","Efficient multi-scale vision transformer (ViT) architecture for dense prediction","Training protocol combining real and synthetic datasets for high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing","Dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps","Zero-shot focal length estimation from a single image","Loss functions and training curriculum promoting sharp depth estimates"],"problems":["Zero-shot monocular depth estimation with metric (absolute scale) outputs on arbitrary images without camera intrinsics","Producing high-resolution, sharp, and fine-grained depth maps that accurately trace object boundaries and fine structures","Achieving low-latency (fast) inference for high-resolution depth maps","Improving boundary accuracy in monocular depth estimation","Accurate focal length estimation from a single image"],"datasets":["AM-2k (Li et al., 2022a)","DIS-.5k (Qin et al., 2022)","Real-world datasets with coarse/inaccurate boundary supervision","Synthetic datasets with accurate pixelwise ground truth"],"metrics":["Boundary recall","Boundary accuracy","Runtime (inference speed)","Metric depth accuracy (absolute scale)"],"results":[{"metric":"Boundary recall","value":"Depth Pro outperforms all prior work by a multiplicative factor"},{"metric":"Runtime","value":"2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a V100 GPU"},{"metric":"Boundary accuracy","value":"Much more accurate boundaries than prior state of the art (e.g., metric monocular depth estimation that produces high-resolution, sharp, and fine-grained depth maps with absolute scale on arbitrary images without camera intrinsics","Efficient multi-scale ViT-based architecture for dense prediction at high resolution and speed","New evaluation metrics leveraging matting datasets for boundary accuracy in depth maps","Zero-shot focal length estimation from a methods: Depth Pro: a foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation
Efficient multi-scale vision transformer (ViT) architecture for dense prediction
Training protocol combining real and synthetic datasets for high metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing
Dedicated evaluation metrics for boundary accuracy in depth maps
Zero-shot focal length estimation from a single image
Loss functions and training curriculum promoting sharp depth estimates
problems: Zero-shot monocular depth estimation with metric (absolute scale) outputs on arbitrary images without camera intrinsics
Producing high-resolution, sharp, and fine-grained depth maps that accurately trace object boundaries and fine structures
Achieving low-latency (fast) inference for high-resolution depth maps
Improving boundary accuracy in monocular depth estimation
Accurate focal length estimation from a single image
datasets: Real-world datasets with coarse/inaccurate boundary supervision
Synthetic datasets with accurate pixelwise ground truth

results.value: Depth Pro outperforms all prior work by a multiplicative factor
2.25-megapixel depth map in 0.3 seconds on a V100 GPU
Much more accurate boundaries than prior state of the art (e.g., Marigold, PatchFusion)
Produces metric depth maps with absolute scale without camera intrinsics

novelty_claims: First foundation model for zero-shot metric monocular depth estimation that produces high-resolution, sharp, and fine-grained depth maps with absolute scale on arbitrary images without camera intrinsics
Efficient multi-scale ViT-based architecture for dense prediction at high resolution and speed
New evaluation metrics leveraging matting datasets for boundary accuracy in depth maps
Zero-shot focal length estimation from a single image that dramatically outperforms prior state of the art
Training protocol combining real and synthetic data for both metric accuracy and fine boundary tracing

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Marigold: a method for affine-invariant monocular depth estimation derived from Stable Diffusion (a latent diffusion model)","Resource-efficient fine-tuning protocol that adapts a pretrained diffusion model (Stable Diffusion) for depth estimation by modifying and fine-tuning only the denoising U-Net, using only synthetic RGB-D data"],"problems":["Monocular depth estimation from a single image, which is geometrically ill-posed and requires strong prior knowledge","Poor generalization of existing monocular depth estimators to images with unfamiliar content and layout (zero-shot generalization)","Resource-intensive training requirements for state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators"],"datasets":["Hypersim","Virtual KITTI"],"metrics":["Affine-invariant depth estimation metrics (e.g., scale-invariant RMSE, relative error, etc.)","Zero-shot generalization performance on real datasets"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance gain on specific datasets","value":"over 20% improvement in some cases"},{"metric":"Zero-shot generalization","value":"State-of-the-art performance on several real datasets without training on real depth maps"}],"novelty_claims":["First to repurpose a pretrained diffusion-based image generator (Stable Diffusion) for monocular depth estimation via a simple fine-tuning protocol.","Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization to real datasets using only synthetic training data.","Resource-efficient: fine-tuning can be done in a couple of days on a single GPU.","Maintains the rich prior knowledge of the diffusion model by only fine-tuning the denoising U-Net."]}

methods: Marigold: a method for affine-invariant monocular depth estimation derived from Stable Diffusion (a latent diffusion model)

Resource-efficient fine-tuning protocol that adapts a pretrained diffusion model (Stable Diffusion) for depth estimation by modifying and fine-tuning only the denoising U-Net, using only synthetic RGB-D data
problems: Monocular depth estimation from a single image, which is geometrically ill-posed and requires strong prior knowledge
Poor generalization of existing monocular depth estimators to images with unfamiliar content and layout (zero-shot generalization)
Resource-intensive training requirements for state-of-the-art monocular depth estimators
metrics: Affine-invariant depth estimation metrics (e.g., scale-invariant RMSE, relative error, etc.)
Zero-shot generalization performance on real datasets

results.value: over 20% improvement in some cases
State-of-the-art performance on several real datasets without training on real depth maps

novelty_claims: First to repurpose a pretrained diffusion-based image generator (Stable Diffusion) for monocular depth estimation via a simple fine-tuning protocol.
Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization to real datasets using only synthetic training data.
Resource-efficient: fine-tuning can be done in a couple of days on a single GPU.
Maintains the rich prior knowledge of the diffusion model by only fine-tuning the denoising U-Net.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["UniDepth model for universal monocular metric depth estimation","Self-promptable camera module predicting dense camera representation to condition depth features","Pseudo-spherical output representation to disentangle camera and depth representations","Geometric invariance loss to promote invariance of camera-prompted depth features"],"problems":["Poor generalization of monocular metric depth estimation (MMDE) methods to unseen domains and cameras","Dependence of existing MMDE methods on camera intrinsics and scene-specific information at test time","Need for universal MMDE that works across arbitrary scenes and cameras without extra information"],"datasets":["KITTI","(plus nine other datasets, not named explicitly in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Zero-shot performance (generalization)","KITTI Depth Prediction Benchmark ranking","Pixel-wise depth estimation accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"KITTI Depth Prediction Benchmark ranking","value":"1st (state of the art)"},{"metric":"Zero-shot performance across 10 datasets","value":"Consistently superior to previous methods, including those trained on the test domains"}],"novelty_claims":["First universal MMDE method that predicts metric 3D points for each pixel from a single image without any additional information (e.g., camera parameters)","Introduction of a promptable camera module that learns a dense, non-parametric camera representation for conditioning depth features","Proposal of a pseudo-spherical output representation to orthogonally separate camera and depth information","Introduction of a geometric invariance loss to disentangle camera and depth features and improve robustness","Extensive zero-shot evaluation across ten datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods even when they are trained on the test domains"]}

methods: UniDepth model for universal monocular metric depth estimation
Self-promptable camera module predicting dense camera representation to condition depth features
Pseudo-spherical output representation to disentangle camera and depth representations
Geometric invariance loss to promote invariance of camera-prompted depth features
problems: Poor generalization of monocular metric depth estimation (MMDE) methods to unseen domains and cameras
Dependence of existing MMDE methods on camera intrinsics and scene-specific information at test time
Need for universal MMDE that works across arbitrary scenes and cameras without extra information
datasets: (plus nine other datasets, not named explicitly in the provided text)

results.value: 1st (state of the art)
Consistently superior to previous methods, including those trained on the test domains

novelty_claims: First universal MMDE method that predicts metric 3D points for each pixel from a single image without any additional information (e.g., camera parameters)
Introduction of a promptable camera module that learns a dense, non-parametric camera representation for conditioning depth features
Proposal of a pseudo-spherical output representation to orthogonally separate camera and depth information
Introduction of a geometric invariance loss to disentangle camera and depth features and improve robustness
Extensive zero-shot evaluation across ten datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods even when they are trained on the test domains

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PatchRefiner framework: a tile-based, patch-wise refinement approach for high-resolution depth estimation","Frozen coarse depth model with residual depth prediction for refinement","Teacher-student framework leveraging synthetic data for pseudo-labeling real data","Detail and Scale Disentangling (DSD) loss integrating ranking supervision and scale-shift invariance"],"problems":["Accurate high-resolution monocular metric depth estimation in real-world scenarios","Scarcity of high-quality, high-resolution real-world depth datasets","Poor generalization from synthetic to real domain due to domain shift and scale differences","Existing methods' inability to capture both sharp details and accurate scale in real-world depth estimation"],"datasets":["Unreal4KStereo","CityScape","ScanNet++","ETH3D"],"metrics":["Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)","Relative Error (REL)","Boundary Recall"],"results":[{"metric":"RMSE (Unreal4KStereo)","value":"18.1% reduction over state-of-the-art"},{"metric":"REL (Unreal4KStereo)","value":"15.7% reduction over state-of-the-art"},{"metric":"Boundary Recall (CityScape)","value":"19.2% increase"}],"novelty_claims":["Reformulation of high-resolution depth estimation as a patch-wise refinement process using a frozen coarse model and residual prediction","Introduction of a teacher-student framework that leverages synthetic data for pseudo-labeling real data, improving detail and scale accuracy","Development of the Detail and Scale Disentangling (DSD) loss to disentangle detail and scale, enabling effective transfer from synthetic to real data","Significant improvements in both detail accuracy and scale estimation across diverse real-world datasets"]}

methods: PatchRefiner framework: a tile-based, patch-wise refinement approach for high-resolution depth estimation
Frozen coarse depth model with residual depth prediction for refinement
Teacher-student framework leveraging synthetic data for pseudo-labeling real data
Detail and Scale Disentangling (DSD) loss integrating ranking supervision and scale-shift invariance
problems: Accurate high-resolution monocular metric depth estimation in real-world scenarios
Scarcity of high-quality, high-resolution real-world depth datasets
Poor generalization from synthetic to real domain due to domain shift and scale differences
Existing methods' inability to capture both sharp details and accurate scale in real-world depth estimation

results.value: 18.1% reduction over state-of-the-art
15.7% reduction over state-of-the-art
19.2% increase

novelty_claims: Reformulation of high-resolution depth estimation as a patch-wise refinement process using a frozen coarse model and residual prediction
Introduction of a teacher-student framework that leverages synthetic data for pseudo-labeling real data, improving detail and scale accuracy
Development of the Detail and Scale Disentangling (DSD) loss to disentangle detail and scale, enabling effective transfer from synthetic to real data
Significant improvements in both detail accuracy and scale estimation across diverse real-world datasets

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["DMD (Diffusion for Metric Depth): a denoising diffusion model for zero-shot metric depth estimation","Log-scale depth parameterization for joint indoor/outdoor modeling","Field-of-view (FOV) conditioning during training and inference to resolve scale ambiguity","Synthetic FOV augmentation during training to generalize to unseen camera intrinsics","Efficient v-parameterization in diffusion for faster inference","Training on a more diverse mixture of datasets than prior work"],"problems":["Zero-shot metric depth estimation from a single image","Joint modeling of indoor and outdoor scenes with different RGB and depth distributions","Depth-scale ambiguity due to unknown camera intrinsics","Generalization to out-of-distribution data and camera intrinsics"],"datasets":["Eight zero-shot (out-of-distribution) benchmarks from ZoeDepth [5]","Two in-distribution benchmarks (not named explicitly, but referenced as in ZoeDepth [5])"],"metrics":["Relative error (REL)"],"results":[{"metric":"Relative error (REL) reduction on zero-shot indoor datasets","value":"25% lower than ZoeDepth"},{"metric":"Relative error (REL) reduction on zero-shot outdoor datasets","value":"33% lower than ZoeDepth"}],"novelty_claims":["First to use a generic, task-agnostic diffusion model for zero-shot metric depth estimation across both indoor and outdoor scenes","Introduction of log-scale depth parameterization for balanced modeling of indoor and outdoor scenes","Field-of-view (FOV) conditioning and synthetic FOV augmentation to address scale ambiguity and generalization to unseen camera intrinsics","Use of v-parameterization in diffusion for efficient inference","Establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) on zero-shot metric depth estimation, outperforming ZoeDepth by a significant margin"]}

methods: DMD (Diffusion for Metric Depth): a denoising diffusion model for zero-shot metric depth estimation
Log-scale depth parameterization for joint indoor/outdoor modeling
Field-of-view (FOV) conditioning during training and inference to resolve scale ambiguity
Synthetic FOV augmentation during training to generalize to unseen camera intrinsics
Efficient v-parameterization in diffusion for faster inference
Training on a more diverse mixture of datasets than prior work
problems: Zero-shot metric depth estimation from a single image
Joint modeling of indoor and outdoor scenes with different RGB and depth distributions
Depth-scale ambiguity due to unknown camera intrinsics
Generalization to out-of-distribution data and camera intrinsics
datasets: Two in-distribution benchmarks (not named explicitly, but referenced as in ZoeDepth [5])

results.metric: Relative error (REL) reduction on zero-shot indoor datasets
results.value: 25% lower than ZoeDepth

results.metric: Relative error (REL) reduction on zero-shot outdoor datasets
results.value: 33% lower than ZoeDepth

novelty_claims: First to use a generic, task-agnostic diffusion model for zero-shot metric depth estimation across both indoor and outdoor scenes

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PatchFusion: a tile-based framework for high-resolution monocular metric depth estimation","Patch-wise fusion network that fuses globally-consistent coarse prediction with finer, inconsistent tiled predictions via high-level feature guidance","Global-to-Local (G2L) module to add context to the fusion network and eliminate patch selection heuristics","Consistency-Aware Training (CAT) and Inference (CAI) to enforce patch overlap consistency and remove need for post-processing"],"problems":["High-resolution monocular metric depth estimation from a single image","Limitations of existing depth estimation models in handling high-resolution inputs","Error propagation and loss of high-frequency details in current high-resolution depth estimation strategies","Scale inconsistencies and lack of global context in previous tile-based methods"],"datasets":["UnrealStereo4K","MVS-Synth","Middleburry 2014"],"metrics":["Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)"],"results":[{"metric":"RMSE improvement on UnrealStereo4K","value":"17.3% improvement over SOTA"},{"metric":"RMSE improvement on MVS-Synth","value":"29.4% improvement over SOTA"}],"novelty_claims":["First end-to-end tile-based metric depth estimation approach that can be trained without pre-processing or post-processing steps","PatchFusion introduces a patch-wise fusion network with high-level feature guidance for improved training and inference","Global-to-Local (G2L) module provides context to the fusion network, removing the need for heuristic patch selection","Consistency-Aware Training and Inference (CAT/CAI) ensures patch overlap consistency, eliminating the need for post-processing corrections such as scale optimization and Gaussian blending","PatchFusion is independent of the base model for depth estimation and can be built on top of existing SOTA models like ZoeDepth"]}

methods: PatchFusion: a tile-based framework for high-resolution monocular metric depth estimation
Patch-wise fusion network that fuses globally-consistent coarse prediction with finer, inconsistent tiled predictions via high-level feature guidance
Global-to-Local (G2L) module to add context to the fusion network and eliminate patch selection heuristics
Consistency-Aware Training (CAT) and Inference (CAI) to enforce patch overlap consistency and remove need for post-processing
problems: High-resolution monocular metric depth estimation from a single image
Limitations of existing depth estimation models in handling high-resolution inputs
Error propagation and loss of high-frequency details in current high-resolution depth estimation strategies
Scale inconsistencies and lack of global context in previous tile-based methods

results.value: 17.3% improvement over SOTA
29.4% improvement over SOTA

novelty_claims: First end-to-end tile-based metric depth estimation approach that can be trained without pre-processing or post-processing steps
PatchFusion introduces a patch-wise fusion network with high-level feature guidance for improved training and inference
Global-to-Local (G2L) module provides context to the fusion network, removing the need for heuristic patch selection
Consistency-Aware Training and Inference (CAT/CAI) ensures patch overlap consistency, eliminating the need for post-processing corrections such as scale optimization and Gaussian blending
PatchFusion is independent of the base model for depth estimation and can be built on top of existing SOTA models like ZoeDepth

methods: Canonical camera space transformation module for metric depth estimation
Random proposal normalization loss for depth accuracy
Joint depth-normal optimization module for surface normal estimation using depth supervision
Large-scale training on 16 million images from diverse datasets and camera models

novelty_claims: First approach to combine relative and metric depth estimation for single-image depth estimation, achieving both generalization and metric scale.
Introduction of a two-stage training framework: relative depth pre-training on diverse datasets, followed by metric fine-tuning with lightweight domain-specific heads.
Novel metric bins module with attractors for per-pixel depth estimation.
Automatic routing of images to the appropriate metric head using a latent classifier during inference.
First model to jointly train on multiple metric datasets (NYU Depth v2 and KITTI) without significant performance drop.
Unprecedented zero-shot generalization to multiple unseen datasets in both indoor and outdoor domains.

methods: Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT): an encoder-decoder architecture using a Vision Transformer (ViT) as the encoder/backbone and a convolutional decoder for dense prediction tasks
Reassembling ViT tokens into image-like feature representations at multiple resolutions
Progressive combination of multi-resolution features into full-resolution predictions
problems: Improving dense prediction tasks (such as monocular depth estimation and semantic segmentation) by overcoming the limitations of convolutional backbones, particularly the loss of feature granularity and limited receptive field

Achieves new state-of-the-art results on multiple dense prediction benchmarks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["A learning framework for geometry-preserving depth estimation using differentiable rendering","Mix-dataset training without extra data or annotations","Novel view synthesis via differentiable rendering of reconstructed 3D point clouds","Consistency loss functions to enforce depth prediction consistency across rendered views","Self-supervised recovery of domain-specific scale and shift coefficients using unlabelled images","Self-supervised estimation of camera intrinsic parameters by minimizing consistency losses"],"problems":["Accurate 3D scene structure recovery from monocular depth estimation","Generalization of depth estimation models across diverse scenes without requiring extra 3D datasets or geometry-complete depth annotations","Overcoming the scale-and-shift ambiguity in mix-dataset trained depth estimators that hinders 3D reconstruction","Producing geometry-preserving depth predictions from monocular images"],"datasets":["Multiple benchmark datasets (not explicitly named in the provided content, but references to Midas and HDN suggest datasets like NYU Depth v2, KITTI, etc.)"],"metrics":["Quantitative scene structure recovery metrics (not explicitly named in the provided content, but likely include RMSE, Abs Rel, etc.)","Qualitative evaluation of 3D structure recovery"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a depth estimation framework that produces geometry-preserving depth without extra datasets or annotations.","Introduces a consistency loss based on differentiable rendering to recover domain-specific affine coefficients in a self-supervised manner using only unlabelled images.","Demonstrates that the self-supervised loss can be used to estimate camera intrinsic parameters.","Shows superior generalization and scene structure recovery on multiple benchmark datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods, without leveraging extra training information."]}

methods: A learning framework for geometry-preserving depth estimation using differentiable rendering
Mix-dataset training without extra data or annotations
Novel view synthesis via differentiable rendering of reconstructed 3D point clouds
Consistency loss functions to enforce depth prediction consistency across rendered views
Self-supervised recovery of domain-specific scale and shift coefficients using unlabelled images
Self-supervised estimation of camera intrinsic parameters by minimizing consistency losses
problems: Accurate 3D scene structure recovery from monocular depth estimation
Generalization of depth estimation models across diverse scenes without requiring extra 3D datasets or geometry-complete depth annotations
Overcoming the scale-and-shift ambiguity in mix-dataset trained depth estimators that hinders 3D reconstruction
Producing geometry-preserving depth predictions from monocular images
datasets: Multiple benchmark datasets (not explicitly named in the provided content, but references to Midas and HDN suggest datasets like NYU Depth v2, KITTI, etc.)
metrics: Quantitative scene structure recovery metrics (not explicitly named in the provided content, but likely include RMSE, Abs Rel, etc.)
novelty_claims: Proposes a depth estimation framework that produces geometry-preserving depth without extra datasets or annotations.
Introduces a consistency loss based on differentiable rendering to recover domain-specific affine coefficients in a self-supervised manner using only unlabelled images.
Demonstrates that the self-supervised loss can be used to estimate camera intrinsic parameters.
Shows superior generalization and scene structure recovery on multiple benchmark datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods, without leveraging extra training information.

methods: SharpNet: a deep network for monocular depth estimation with explicit constraints for occluding contours and surface normals
Multi-task learning to predict depths, normals, and occluding contours from a single image
Novel loss terms that constrain depth predictions with occluding contours and normals during training
Pretraining on synthetic images with perfect occluding contour annotations, followed by fine-tuning on real data (NYUv2-Depth)
problems: Accurate monocular depth estimation from a single RGB image
Precise reconstruction of occluding contours in depth maps, which are challenging due to annotation noise and their small proportion in images
Improving the realism of virtual object integration in augmented reality by enhancing occluding contour accuracy
datasets: Synthetic dataset with object instance boundaries for pretraining
NYUv2-OC: a manually annotated subset of 100 NYUv2-Depth images with ground truth occluding contours
metrics: Standard depth estimation metrics (e.g., RMSE, absolute relative error, etc.)

results.value: Outperforms all previous methods, including the current leading monocular depth estimation method
Places second on all standard benchmarks, on par with state-of-the-art methods

novelty_claims: Introduction of SharpNet, a method that explicitly constrains depth, normals, and occluding contours during training for improved monocular depth estimation
First to introduce a loss term that directly constrains depth predictions with occluding contours, leading to sharper and more accurate contour reconstruction
Demonstrates that accuracy along occluding contours can surpass that of structured-light depth cameras
Creation and public release of NYUv2-OC, a new benchmark with manually annotated occluding contours for fair evaluation
Shows that the improvement in occluding contour accuracy is not merely due to multi-task learning, but specifically from the new constraint term

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Canonical camera space transformation module (canonical and de-canonical camera transformation)","Random proposal normalization loss","Large-scale training on 8 million images from 11 datasets with diverse camera models"],"problems":["Zero-shot metric 3D prediction from a single image","Metric ambiguity in monocular depth estimation due to varying camera models","Lack of generalization of metric depth models to unseen cameras and scenes","Inability to recover real-world metric scale from single images in the wild"],"datasets":["11 datasets (not all named, but includes both indoor and outdoor scenes, and diverse camera models)","Benchmarks from the 2nd Monocular Depth Estimation Challenge"],"metrics":["Zero-shot generalization performance on 7 benchmarks","Depth estimation accuracy (metric depth error)","Performance in downstream tasks (e.g., scale drift in monocular SLAM)"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot benchmark performance","value":"State-of-the-art (SOTA) on 7 benchmarks"},{"metric":"2nd Monocular Depth Estimation Challenge","value":"1st place (championship)"},{"metric":"Scale drift in monocular SLAM","value":"Significantly reduced (qualitative improvement)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a canonical and de-canonical camera transformation method to resolve metric depth ambiguity from various camera settings, enabling zero-shot monocular metric depth learning from large-scale datasets.","Introduces a random proposal normalization loss to boost depth accuracy by emphasizing local geometry and distribution.","Demonstrates the first zero-shot, single-view, metric depth model that generalizes to in-the-wild images with unseen camera settings and enables accurate metric 3D structure recovery from internet images.","Shows that the method can be plugged into existing monocular models and directly benefits downstream tasks such as monocular SLAM and 3D scene reconstruction."]}

methods: Canonical camera space transformation module (canonical and de-canonical camera transformation)
Random proposal normalization loss
Large-scale training on 8 million images from 11 datasets with diverse camera models
problems: Zero-shot metric 3D prediction from a single image
Metric ambiguity in monocular depth estimation due to varying camera models
Lack of generalization of metric depth models to unseen cameras and scenes
Inability to recover real-world metric scale from single images in the wild
datasets: 11 datasets (not all named, but includes both indoor and outdoor scenes, and diverse camera models)
Benchmarks from the 2nd Monocular Depth Estimation Challenge
metrics: Performance in downstream tasks (e.g., scale drift in monocular SLAM)

results.value: State-of-the-art (SOTA) on 7 benchmarks
1st place (championship)
Significantly reduced (qualitative improvement)

novelty_claims: Proposes a canonical and de-canonical camera transformation method to resolve metric depth ambiguity from various camera settings, enabling zero-shot monocular metric depth learning from large-scale datasets.
Introduces a random proposal normalization loss to boost depth accuracy by emphasizing local geometry and distribution.

Demonstrates the first zero-shot, single-view, metric depth model that generalizes to in-the-wild images with unseen camera settings and enables accurate metric 3D structure recovery from internet images.
Shows that the method can be plugged into existing monocular models and directly benefits downstream tasks such as monocular SLAM and 3D scene reconstruction.

methods: Replacing all labeled real images with synthetic images for training
Scaling up the capacity of the teacher model trained on synthetic images
Teaching student models via large-scale pseudo-labeled real images generated by the teacher model
Offering models of different scales (25M to 1.3B parameters)
Fine-tuning models with metric depth labels to obtain metric depth models
Constructing a new versatile evaluation benchmark with precise annotations and diverse scenes
problems: Improving the accuracy and robustness of monocular depth estimation models
Addressing the limitations of real image labels (noise, limited diversity) in training data
Overcoming the weaknesses of discriminative models in capturing fine details and handling transparent/reflective objects
Providing efficient and scalable depth estimation models for various applications
Addressing the lack of diverse and precise evaluation benchmarks for monocular depth estimation
datasets: Large-scale pseudo-labeled real images (generated by the teacher model)
Newly constructed versatile evaluation benchmark with precise annotations and diverse scenes

results.value: More than 10x faster than latest Stable Diffusion-based models
Significantly more accurate than latest Stable Diffusion-based models

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that efficient discriminative models can achieve extremely fine details in depth estimation by using synthetic images for training, without the need for diffusion-based models.
Introduces a training paradigm that scales up a teacher model on synthetic data and distills knowledge to student models via pseudo-labeled real images.
Constructs a new versatile evaluation benchmark with precise annotations and diverse scenes to better reflect model strengths and facilitate future research.
Offers a family of models with varying scales (25M to 1.3B parameters) to support a wide range of applications.
Shows that data quality (synthetic vs. real) is more critical than model architecture for achieving high-quality monocular depth estimation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Encoder-decoder convolutional neural network baseline","Transformer-based architecture block (AdaBins) that adaptively divides the depth range into bins per image","Global statistical analysis and post-processing at high resolution using AdaBins"],"problems":["Estimating high quality dense depth maps from a single RGB image","Improving global information processing in depth estimation networks","Handling varying depth distributions in different images for depth estimation"],"datasets":["NYU Depth v2 (NYU)","KITTI"],"metrics":["Standard depth estimation metrics (e.g., RMSE, Abs Rel, δ1, δ2, δ3)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes AdaBins, an adaptive bin-width estimator block that divides the depth range into bins whose centers are estimated per image, allowing the network to focus on relevant depth regions.","Performs global processing of scene information at high resolution, rather than only at low resolution as in traditional encoder-decoder architectures.","Achieves decisive improvement over state-of-the-art methods on NYU and KITTI datasets across all metrics.","Provides an ablation study to validate the effectiveness of the AdaBins block."]}

methods: Encoder-decoder convolutional neural network baseline
Transformer-based architecture block (AdaBins) that adaptively divides the depth range into bins per image
Global statistical analysis and post-processing at high resolution using AdaBins
problems: Estimating high quality dense depth maps from a single RGB image
Improving global information processing in depth estimation networks
Handling varying depth distributions in different images for depth estimation
metrics: Standard depth estimation metrics (e.g., RMSE, Abs Rel, δ1, δ2, δ3)
novelty_claims: Proposes AdaBins, an adaptive bin-width estimator block that divides the depth range into bins whose centers are estimated per image, allowing the network to focus on relevant depth regions.
Performs global processing of scene information at high resolution, rather than only at low resolution as in traditional encoder-decoder architectures.
Achieves decisive improvement over state-of-the-art methods on NYU and KITTI datasets across all metrics.
Provides an ablation study to validate the effectiveness of the AdaBins block.

methods: Data engine for collecting and automatically annotating large-scale unlabeled images (~62M) for monocular depth estimation
Self-training approach combining 1.5M labeled images and pseudo-labeled images from the data engine
Challenging optimization target using strong data augmentation to encourage robust representation learning from pseudo labels
Auxiliary supervision via feature alignment loss to inherit semantic priors from pre-trained encoders (e.g., DINOv2) instead of traditional semantic segmentation auxiliary tasks
problems: Limited generalization and robustness of monocular depth estimation (MDE) models due to insufficient data coverage
Difficulty in building large-scale labeled depth datasets for MDE
Ineffectiveness of naive self-training with pseudo-labeled data in improving MDE performance

results.value: Stronger than MiDaS-BEiTL-512
Outperforms ZoeDepth significantly on NYUv2 and KITTI (exact numbers not provided)

novelty_claims: First to scale up monocular depth estimation using massive unlabeled data (62M images) with automatic annotation.
Propose a key practice of challenging the model with a harder optimization target (via data augmentation) when learning from pseudo labels, rather than naive self-training.
Introduce auxiliary supervision via feature alignment loss to inherit semantic priors from pre-trained encoders (e.g., DINOv2), instead of using a traditional semantic segmentation auxiliary task.
Demonstrate that the resulting model achieves stronger zero-shot generalization and sets new SOTA after fine-tuning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Creation of two large-scale, diverse self-supervised monocular depth estimation (SS-MDE) datasets: SlowTV and CribsTV","Learning camera intrinsics as part of the model","Aspect ratio augmentation (AR-Aug) to diversify image shapes during training","Stronger photometric augmentation regime, including RandAugment and CutOut","Support frame randomization during training","Flexible motion estimation in the self-supervised pipeline","Use of modern transformer-based depth network architectures (e.g., DPT)","Extensive ablation experiments to evaluate each component"],"problems":["Limited generalization of self-supervised monocular depth estimation (SS-MDE) models due to lack of diverse training data","Over-reliance on urban driving datasets for SS-MDE, leading to poor performance in natural and indoor scenes","Difficulty in scaling supervised monocular depth estimation due to the need for expensive and limited ground-truth annotations","Gap in performance between self-supervised and supervised monocular depth estimation, especially in indoor settings"],"datasets":["SlowTV (1.7M frames from diverse YouTube videos)","CribsTV (330k images from real estate virtual tours)","Mannequin Challenge","KITTI"],"metrics":["Zero-shot generalization performance (across multiple datasets)","Standard monocular depth estimation metrics (e.g., Abs Rel, RMSE, δ thresholds)","Ablation study results (relative performance improvements)"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot generalization (qualitative)","value":"Outperforms all existing SS-MDE approaches and some state-of-the-art supervised methods"},{"metric":"Performance on indoor datasets","value":"Closes the gap between self-supervised and supervised methods; matches or outperforms SotA supervised techniques except DPT-BEiT"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of two large-scale, diverse SS-MDE datasets (SlowTV and CribsTV) sourced from YouTube, enabling training on 2M frames without ground-truth annotations","Demonstration of zero-shot generalization to a wide range of environments, including natural, indoor, and underwater scenes","Incorporation of camera intrinsics learning, aspect ratio augmentation, stronger photometric augmentations, support frame randomization, flexible motion estimation, and transformer-based architectures to maximize generalization","Extensive ablation studies showing the effectiveness of each proposed component","Closing the performance gap between self-supervised and supervised monocular depth estimation, achieving state-of-the-art results in SS-MDE"]}

methods: Creation of two large-scale, diverse self-supervised monocular depth estimation (SS-MDE) datasets: SlowTV and CribsTV
Learning camera intrinsics as part of the model
Aspect ratio augmentation (AR-Aug) to diversify image shapes during training
Stronger photometric augmentation regime, including RandAugment and CutOut
Support frame randomization during training
Flexible motion estimation in the self-supervised pipeline
Use of modern transformer-based depth network architectures (e.g., DPT)
Extensive ablation experiments to evaluate each component
problems: Limited generalization of self-supervised monocular depth estimation (SS-MDE) models due to lack of diverse training data
Over-reliance on urban driving datasets for SS-MDE, leading to poor performance in natural and indoor scenes
Difficulty in scaling supervised monocular depth estimation due to the need for expensive and limited ground-truth annotations
Gap in performance between self-supervised and supervised monocular depth estimation, especially in indoor settings
datasets: SlowTV (1.7M frames from diverse YouTube videos)
CribsTV (330k images from real estate virtual tours)
metrics: Standard monocular depth estimation metrics (e.g., Abs Rel, RMSE, δ thresholds)

results.value: Outperforms all existing SS-MDE approaches and some state-of-the-art supervised methods
Closes the gap between self-supervised and supervised methods; matches or outperforms SotA supervised techniques except DPT-BEiT

novelty_claims: Introduction of two large-scale, diverse SS-MDE datasets (SlowTV and CribsTV) sourced from YouTube, enabling training on 2M frames without ground-truth annotations
Demonstration of zero-shot generalization to a wide range of environments, including natural, indoor, and underwater scenes

Incorporation of camera intrinsics learning, aspect ratio augmentation, stronger photometric augmentations, support frame randomization, flexible motion estimation, and transformer-based architectures to maximize generalization
Extensive ablation studies showing the effectiveness of each proposed component
Closing the performance gap between self-supervised and supervised monocular depth estimation, achieving state-of-the-art results in SS-MDE
